Fear and Greed is at
33
(Fear),
and BTC is at
$73K
to open June. May closed in the red, with BTC down roughly 3.65%
for the month as geopolitical deal optimism, inflation fears, and
record ETF outflows combined to weigh on prices

June opens with a genuine policy shift. The CFTC just authorised
perpetual futures for US traders for the first time. Social
sentiment hit its highest reading of 2026 this week, even as
prices fell. Strategy is signalling another buy. The market is in
extreme fear, but the regulatory calendar just moved
significantly in crypto's favour.

Let's dive in.

Latest News
-----------

Strategy Is Signalling Another Bitcoin Buy

Michael Saylor posted his standard pre-buy chart on May 31
captioned "Working Better," a signal that has preceded every
Strategy Bitcoin purchase for six years. May closed with BTC down
roughly 3.65%.

Any new purchase would fall below Strategy's average acquisition
cost of $75,701 per coin.

What has Strategy's average Bitcoin acquisition cost been across
all purchases?

Read More  
( https://link.coinmarketcap.com/f/a/Yrb3MWEJ01BZgPuEs0kzKw~~/AAQRxRA~/MIlBbauFsMFTX5oakhPWZJNI80VE1XtrikUBU0hzEIoy5xyKh1Jd5V04g_2S3UvUROjWQQFyz9JbK6ePbHYQXhvXNSPhPtRNYCt--0hKIXSTMXzaEPjonClX75dnP3FeYPjJ5pcfD7k9ovT7XO3Hcw2imqd5osiQzFpUyUFmRpc~ )
   Bitcoin Social Sentiment Hit a Year High as Prices Fell

Bullish Bitcoin comments outnumbered bearish ones by 2

73878.subject: Should you use a sleep tracker?
73878.body:
The Economist’s week ahead

Автор: The Economist


Автор: The Economist

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/should-you-use-sleep-tracker-the-economist-ntwre?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BHXMxTRQzSoi65LNLKb5u%2Fg%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=0b3dNYTpKVMsg1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mpv39xv7~q8-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mpv39xv7-q8
  
Enjoying this newsletter? Get it in your inbox every weekend, as well as our daily round-up of The Economist’s best journalism, by signing up for free here. Trackers—smartwatches, smart rings, phone apps—can tell you how much shut-eye you actually got, and perhaps log how long you spent in different sleep stages. But more data has downsides: fretting about rest is one of the most common causes of a sleepless night. Last week ExxonMobil’s shareholders voted to cut ties with New Jersey and reincorporate in Texas. The oil major is only the latest company to shift its headquarters to the Lone Star State. From Dallas’s “Y’all Street” to Austin’s “Silicon Hills”, Texas is steadily establishing itself as America Inc’s new centre of gravity.  What to expect in the week ahead: ▸ Ethiopia heads to the polls on Monday. Abiy Ahmed, the prime minister, is certain to be re-elected. Mr Abiy once promised a historic transition to democracy. Instead, through guile and brute force, he has personalised power to a degree unseen since the country’s last emperor. ▸The euro area’s inflation data for May will be published on Tuesday. Its annual inflation rate has been creeping up this year, hitting 3% in April, as a consequence of higher energy prices induced by the war in Iran. ▸ More American states hold primary elections on Tuesday, including California. Voters will choose the top two candidates for the state’s next governor. Gavin Newsom, the incumbent, cannot run again because of term limits. More than a dozen Democrats are running, among them Tom Steyer, a billionaire. Annie Crabill, Senior digital editor  Editor’s picks Should you use a sleep tracker? Nearly half of all Americans and around 40% of Britons now use a smartwatch, smart ring, phone app or another similar device to track their sleep. Are these gadgets accurate—and do their users get a better night’s rest?  Behold the success of Texan business  “Just when I thought I got him to fall in love with Tennessee, I shoulda known better than to take him back to Abilene.” So croons Ella Langley in “Choosin’ Texas”, a song that has ranked near the top of America’s music charts throughout 2026. Some of those listens may be coming from policymakers elsewhere in the country. Much like Ms Langley, they are losing to Texas.  How should bosses talk about AI? If you are a bank boss and in the headlines, you ar

73876.subject: Everything Anthropic Shipped in 2026: Every Claude Model, Agent, & Tool - And How to Actually Use Them 🤖
73876.body:
View this post on the web at https://linas.substack.com/p/anthropic-claude-2026-every-launch-guide

👋 Hey, Linas here! Every day, I break down 3 stories shaping the future of FinTech & Artificial Intelligence - plus the money movements and trends worth tracking. First time here? 387k+ FinTech and AI leaders get this daily. Join them:
Anthropic shipped a major Claude release roughly every two weeks since January 1, 2026. 29 distinct product updates in a matter of five months 😳
New models, new agents, new desktop apps, new certification programs, multibillion-dollar funding rounds, and a restricted-access AI that cybersecurity firms say rewrites the threat landscape.
Between January and May, Claude went from a chatbot with a text box to an execution platform that writes code across parallel repos, runs autonomously for days on a single /goal command, manages payroll through QuickBooks, and identifies zero-day vulnerabilities hidden for over a decade. Anthropic’s run-rate revenue went from $14 billion to $47 billion in the same period 🤯
That said, the gap between what Claude can do today and what most people are asking it to do is the single largest source of wasted capability in enterprise software.
If you’ve ever been wondering whether you’re getting the most out of Claude right now, the answer is almost certainly NO.
That’s why below is every launch in chronological order, with benchmarks, business context, and a practical “how to get the most out of it” section for each product & feature.
Read it end to end or skip to the product you care about the most. Either way, you’ll leave knowing exactly what changed and what to do differently.
The Scale of the Blitz: 2026 by the Numbers
Before the timeline, let’s look at the context that makes every launch make sense.
The revenue trajectory alone tells an insane story: $1 billion ARR in December 2024, $9 billion by end of 2025, $14 billion in February 2026, $30 billion by April, $47 billion by late May.
No company in the history of B2B software has scaled this fast. Anthropic is basically converting safety-lab credibility into execution-platform dominance at a pace that collapses entire product cycles into weeks.
The Four Modes: What Claude Actually Is Now
Most people still think of Claude as a chatbot. That framing is three-quarters wrong. Claude now operates in four distinct modes, and the mode you choose determines what’s possible.
The one-line rule: Chat for quick questions. Cowork for real work on your files. Code for development. Projects for recurring work with consistent context.
January 2026: The Year Begins With a Structural Shift
January 12 - Anthropic Labs + Claude Cowork Research Preview
On January 11, Anthropic launched Anthropic Labs, an experimental product arm co-led by Instagram co-founder Mike Krieger, built to move fast on consumer product ideas that wouldn’t fit the core research roadmap. The next day, Claude Cowork shipped as a research preview.
Cowork changed th

73873.subject: The end of the monthly AI bill
73873.body:
----------
######                      [Sign Up](http://utm.genai.works/r/gg2ubr5) | [Collaborate with us](https://6hhfxazcbpc.typeform.com/to/uXIl0IFH?utm_source=atlas&utm_medium=newsletter&utm_campaign=general_2026_05_11&utm_content=banner_post)


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/8776fa6e-e2d0-4aec-9648-1bed6de3a659/GenAI_Daily_Banner.jpg?t=1778687445)
Caption: 


----------

----------
**Welcome back!** The AI you use today lives in someone else's data center and charges you by the token. Nvidia's new chip wants to drag that whole brain onto your laptop and end the monthly bill, which is a bigger deal than it sounds. The same hunger for compute is driving the rest of the day. SoftBank is spending $81 billion to turn French nuclear power into AI data centers, and OpenAI is putting its locked biology model in government hands. Ford, meanwhile, became this month's hottest AI stock without earning a cent from the business behind the hype.

———————————————————————————

* **Nvidia:** What changes when AI stops living in the cloud and moves inside your laptop?

## [The end of the monthly AI bill](https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/nvidia-introduces-first-pcs-designed-for-ai-agents-47445bcd)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/4a15d0e1-6122-48d0-aab4-511672676e29/Beehiiv_01_-_Jun_01.png?t=1780305323)
Caption: 

Every time you use AI today, you are renting it. Your words travel to a data center, get processed there, travel back, and you pay for the privilege one token at a time. 

Nvidia wants to end that by putting the whole AI brain inside your computer. 

At Computex in Taiwan, Jensen Huang held up the RTX Spark, a chip built to run AI on your own machine, and said it will change the PC as much as the smartphone changed the phone. 

* It pairs a 20-core Arm CPU with a Blackwell GPU and 128GB of unified memory, with context up to a million tokens.

* It runs Windows on Arm and was built with MediaTek, putting Nvidia up against Intel, AMD, Qualcomm and Apple in PCs for the first time.

* Dell, HP, Lenovo, Microsoft, Asus and MSI ship RTX Spark machines this fall, with Acer and Gigabyte to follow.

* In plain terms, a powerful AI model that used to run only in the cloud can now live on your laptop, answer instantly, keep your files private and never run up a cloud bill.

Every major PC maker lined up behind one chip, which tells you they believe the next computer is one that runs it
